attributes.stem:
A 73-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department with a two-week history of worsening shortness of breath and peripheral oedema. He has a medical history of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction diagnosed one year ago, bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome requiring surgery, and spinal stenosis. His medications include furosemide, spironolactone, and ramipril. He has had three hospital admissions in the past six months for heart failure.

On examination, he appears short of breath at rest with a pulse rate of 88/min, blood pressure 105/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min, SpO2 93% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. He has bilateral ankle oedema, bibasal crackles on chest auscultation, mild periorbital bruising, and his tongue appears slightly enlarged.

Investigations reveal troponin I 0.15 ng/mL (0.00-0.04), N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide 5600 pg/mL (0-125), and normal renal function. The electrocardiogram shows low-voltage QRS complexes in the limb leads.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features highly suggestive of cardiac amyloidosis, an infiltrative cardiomyopathy characterised by the deposition of amyloid fibrils in the heart tissue. The clinical presentation includes heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, intolerance to standard heart failure medications (particularly ACE inhibitors due to autonomic dysfunction), and extracardiac manifestations.

Cardiac amyloidosis is the most likely diagnosis based on several characteristic findings: low voltage ECG despite increased wall thickness (voltage-mass mismatch), preserved ejection fraction, and recurrent heart failure admissions. The patient also has several extracardiac manifestations typical of amyloidosis including bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, macroglossia (enlarged tongue), and periorbital bruising (raccoon eyes). The disproportionate elevation of troponin despite no acute coronary syndrome is another red flag for cardiac amyloidosis. Histologically, amyloidosis is diagnosed by demonstrating amyloid deposits in tissue biopsies that stain with Congo red and show characteristic apple-green birefringence under polarised light microscopy.

Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy typically presents with asymmetric septal hypertrophy. Additionally, the ECG in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy usually shows high voltage QRS complexes (not low voltage), and the extracardiac manifestations seen in this patient (carpal tunnel syndrome, macroglossia, periorbital bruising) are not features of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy.

Constrictive pericarditis can present with heart failure symptoms and preserved ejection fraction, but it would show a thickened pericardium rather than increased myocardial wall thickness. The extracardiac manifestations are not consistent with constrictive pericarditis.

Fabry disease is an X-linked lysosomal storage disorder that can cause left ventricular hypertrophy, but it typically presents at a younger age and is associated with other manifestations such as angiokeratomas, corneal opacities, and neuropathic pain. The low voltage ECG and extracardiac manifestations in this patient are more consistent with amyloidosis than Fabry disease.

Sarcoidosis can affect the heart and cause cardiomyopathy, but cardiac sarcoidosis more commonly presents with conduction abnormalities, ventricular arrhythmias, and patchy myocardial involvement rather than the diffuse infiltration seen in amyloidosis. The extracardiac manifestations in this patient are not typical of sarcoidosis, which more commonly affects the lungs, lymph nodes, and skin.

Cardiac amyloidosis is an underdiagnosed cause of heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, particularly in older adults. The two main types are light chain (AL) amyloidosis, associated with plasma cell dyscrasias, and transthyretin (ATTR) amyloidosis, which can be either hereditary or wild-type (formerly called senile amyloidosis). Diagnosis is confirmed by tissue biopsy (either cardiac or extracardiac) and typing of the amyloid protein. The pathognomonic histological finding is the presence of amyloid deposits that stain with Congo red and demonstrate apple-green birefringence when viewed under polarised light microscopy.
learningPoints: Cardiac amyloidosis typically presents with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, low voltage ECG despite increased wall thickness, and intolerance to standard heart failure medications.
Red flags for cardiac amyloidosis include bilateral carpal tunnel syndrome, periorbital bruising, macroglossia, and autonomic symptoms.
Diagnosis of amyloidosis is confirmed by tissue biopsy showing Congo red staining with apple-green birefringence under polarised light.
references: Naing P, Forrester D, Kangaharan N, Muthumala A, Mon Myint S, Playford D. Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction: A growing global epidemic. Aust J Gen Pract. 2019;48(7):465-471. Available from: https://www1.racgp.org.au/ajgp/2019/july/heart-failure-with-preserved-ejection-fraction
